title: Lemon Sorbet
subtitle: Fresh lemon juice churned with a light sugar syrup into a clean, icy sorbet
excerpt: Freshly squeezed lemon juice combined with a simple sugar syrup and churned in an ice cream maker until smooth and pale yellow. No cream, no eggs, no dairy. The result is intensely lemon-flavoured, clean, and sharply refreshing. A palate cleanser or a light dessert.
sourceNotes: Lemon sorbet belongs to the tradition of water ices that developed in seventeenth and eighteenth century Europe from Italian granita origins. The modern sorbet, churned in a machine to a smooth texture, became a standard item in French restaurant cooking as a palate cleanser between courses.

content.text: Sorbet is mostly water, so getting the sugar balance right matters more than in ice cream. Too little sugar and the sorbet freezes rock-hard; too much and it won't freeze properly and stays slushy. The ratio here is tested: it gives a smooth, scoopable sorbet at -18°C. The lemon zest goes into the sugar syrup during cooking to extract its oils, then is strained out.
The syrup and juice must be cold before churning. Churn time is typically 20, 25 minutes; the sorbet should look smooth, pale, and hold its shape when you take a spoonful.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: finely grated zest and juice (about 200 ml juice)

content.text: You will also need 400 ml of cold water for the syrup.

Combine {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, 400 ml of cold water, and the lemon zest in a medium saucepan. Heat over medium heat, stirring, until the sugar dissolves. Bring just to the boil, then remove from the heat. Strain through a fine sieve to remove the zest and leave to cool completely. Refrigerate until cold.

Stir the freshly squeezed juice of {{lemon}} into the cold syrup. Churn in an ice cream maker according to the manufacturer's instructions until smooth and pale, about 20, 25 minutes. Transfer to a lidded freezer-proof container and freeze for at least 2 hours to harden.

items.fix: Transfer to the fridge for 15 minutes before scooping. For next time, the syrup-to-juice ratio must be accurate, sorbet needs more sugar than ice cream to stay scoopable.
items.symptom: Sorbet is rock hard and won't scoop

items.fix: Churn for longer until the texture is clearly pale and smooth. If the sorbet won't firm up, refreeze for 2 hours between churn and serving.
items.cause: Too much sugar in the syrup, or not churned long enough
items.symptom: Sorbet is slushy and won't hold a scoop

items.fix: Zest only the outer yellow layer, stopping before the white pith. The pith is bitter. Using a microplane grater makes this easier.
items.cause: Pith was included when zesting the lemons
items.symptom: Sorbet is bitter rather than sharp

items.fix: Make sure the syrup is fridge-cold before churning. Warm liquid going into the machine warms the bowl and gives a larger ice crystal.
items.cause: Syrup was not cold before churning
items.symptom: Sorbet has a grainy, icy texture

content.text: Lemon sorbet belongs to the tradition of European water ices that began with Italian granitas and developed into the churned sorbet of French haute cuisine. The French sorbet became a standard restaurant course, served between fish and meat courses as a palate cleanser, in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The modern lemon sorbet is made exactly the same way: a light sugar syrup combined with acid fruit juice and churned until smooth. It has no dairy, no eggs, and no special technique beyond the machine.
